title: The Shaman's Sickness: Kundalini as Initiation Into Service
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Staring at the screen, probably in the dark, the clock mocking you. Your body feels like it's betraying you, your mind a battlefie
body:
The Shaman's Sickness: Kundalini as Initiation Into Service

You're here, aren't you? Staring at the screen, probably in the dark, the clock mocking you. Your body feels like it's betraying you, your mind a battlefield. You're scared. Alone. And you're wondering if you're losing your mind. I've been there. That gnawing fear, the physical sensations that make no sense, the feeling like you've been dropped into a different reality and forgotten the instruction manual. That's what brings most of us to this topic - Kundalini, shamanic sickness, spiritual emergency. Whatever you call it, it's a hell of a ride, and you're not crazy. You're just being initiated.

I know what you're thinking. "Initiated into what? More pain? More confusion?" Believe me, I get it. When I was going through it, all I wanted was for it to stop. The burning, the shaking, the electric currents, the visions that wouldn't quit. My body felt like it was plugged into a faulty circuit, my mind racing with thoughts that weren't mine. I thought I was dying, or worse, going insane. And the doctors? They didn't have a clue. They just looked at me with that pitying stare, offered me pills, and sent me on my way. Does that sound familiar? Right?

But here's the truth: what you're experiencing, as terrifying and isolating as it feels, has a history. It has a context. It's not a random breakdown. It's a breakthrough. It's an ancient process, known across cultures, often called the "shaman's sickness." You're not broken; you're being remade. And that's a hard pill to swallow when every fiber of your being is screaming for normalcy.

What the Hell is Happening to Me?

Let's get down to brass tacks. You're experiencing something big. Maybe you meditated too hard, maybe you had a spontaneous awakening, maybe it was a trauma, or even drugs. It doesn't really matter how it started right now. What matters is what's happening. You've stumbled into the activation of Kundalini, a Sanskrit term for a dormant spiritual energy coiled at the base of your spine. It's often described as a sleeping serpent. When it wakes up, it moves through your central nervous system, clearing out blockages, purifying your system, and literally rewiring you. And it's not a gentle process. It's a force of nature.

Think about it like this: your entire being - physical, emotional, mental, spiritual - is undergoing a radical upgrade. Your old operating system can't handle the new software. So, it crashes. Hard. That's why you feel so disoriented, so raw. Your senses are heightened, your emotions are all over the place, your thoughts are chaotic, and your body is reacting in ways you never thought possible. It's not imaginary. It's real. And it's intense.

Physical Symptoms You Can't Ignore

You're probably experiencing a whole laundry list of physical weirdness. I know I did. You might have:

  • Intense heat or cold sensations, sometimes localized, sometimes all over.
  • Vibrations, tremors, or involuntary movements (kriyas).
  • Burning sensations, especially in the spine or head.
  • Heart palpitations, chest pressure, difficulty breathing.
  • Digestive issues, nausea, loss of appetite.
  • Muscle spasms, aches, and pains that move around.
  • Changes in vision or hearing - seeing auras, hearing buzzing or internal sounds.
  • Insomnia or drastically altered sleep patterns.
  • Headaches, pressure in the head, tingling in the scalp.

You've probably been to doctors, right? They run tests, scratch their heads, and tell you everything's "normal." Or they suggest anxiety, depression, or even psychosis. And while mental health is absolutely something to keep an eye on, what you're feeling isn't just anxiety. It's a deep, systemic shift. This energy is literally moving through your body, clearing out old traumas, old patterns, old energies that have been stuck. It's like your body is a house being renovated, and all the old junk is being ripped out and thrown around. It's messy, it's loud, and it feels destructive. But it's also clearing the way for something new.

The Mental and Emotional Rollercoaster

Oh, the mind. This is where it gets really fun, isn't it? One minute you're fine, the next you're plunged into despair. You might be experiencing:

  • Intense fear, anxiety, panic attacks that come out of nowhere.
  • Spontaneous memories, flashbacks, or unresolved traumas bubbling up.
  • Periods of intense depression, apathy, or emotional numbness.
  • Overwhelm, confusion, difficulty concentrating.
  • Sensory overload - lights too bright, sounds too loud, people too much.
  • Feelings of depersonalization or derealization - like you're not real, or the world isn't real.
  • Visions, auditory hallucinations, or psychic experiences that feel unsettling.
  • A deep sense of isolation and loneliness, even when surrounded by people.

This is the "shaman's sickness" part. You're being stripped bare. Your ego, your old identity, your comfortable view of the world - it's all being dismantled. This can feel like a death. And in a way, it is. The old you is dying to make way for a new you. This process can bring up all your deepest fears, your unhealed wounds, everything you've ever suppressed. It's like a spiritual purification, and it's not for the faint of heart. But here's the thing: you're still here. You're still breathing. That means you're strong enough to get through this. You just don't know it yet.

It's Not a Curse, It's an Initiation

Look, I know it feels like a curse right now. Like you did something wrong, or you're being punished. But this isn't punishment. This is training. This is an initiation. In many indigenous cultures, a shaman doesn't choose their path; the path chooses them. They often experience a period of intense illness, psychological breakdown, visions, and disorientation - the "shaman's sickness" - before emerging with new abilities to heal, to see, to serve their community. You're going through your own version of that. You're being called to something bigger than yourself.

You're developing sensitivities, intuitions, and an understanding of reality that goes beyond the mundane. You're being given a deeper connection to the spiritual realms, whether you asked for it or not. And yes, it's messy. It's terrifying. It's isolating. Because most people around you won't understand. They can't. They haven't been through it. But you're not alone in the experience itself. Many have walked this path before you, and many are walking it now.

One book that really helped me understand the process was The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). Reading about other people's experiences, how similar they were to mine, it was like a lifeline. It made me feel less insane. Another incredibly helpful resource, particularly for understanding the broader context of spiritual crises, is The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). It puts what you're going through into a approach of spiritual emergency, not just mental illness. It helped me see the purpose in the pain.

Surviving the Storm: What You Can Do

So, you're in the thick of it. What do you do? How do you survive? How do you come out the other side not just intact, but stronger? Here's what I learned, often the hard way:

Grounding, Grounding, Grounding

This is your anchor. When you feel like you're floating away, or your body is too electric, you need to ground yourself. This isn't some woo-woo concept; it's about connecting to the physical world, to the earth, to your body.

  • Nature: Get outside. Put your bare feet on the grass or dirt. Sit under a tree. Let the earth absorb some of that excess energy.
  • Movement: Gentle movement, not intense exercise. Walking, slow stretching, tai chi, gentle yoga. Get that energy moving through your body in a controlled way.
  • Food: Eat nourishing, grounding foods. Root vegetables, healthy fats, warm meals. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar. Your body needs fuel, not more agitation.
  • Water: Drink plenty of water. Take warm baths with Epsom salts. Water is incredibly cleansing and soothing.
  • Presence: Focus on your senses. What do you see, hear, smell, taste, touch? Bring yourself back to the present moment, away from the swirling chaos in your head.

Honestly, when I was at my worst, just lying on the floor and feeling the solid ground beneath me was enough to pull me back from the brink. It won't stop it, but it helps you ride the waves.

Seek Support (The Right Kind)

This is crucial. You can't do this alone. But you need the right kind of support.

  • Therapists familiar with spiritual emergency: This is key. A regular therapist might pathologize your experiences. Look for someone who understands transpersonal psychology, spiritual emergence, or shamanic crises. Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) is a fantastic book that can help you understand what to look for in a professional.
  • Kundalini-aware practitioners: Some healers, energy workers, or spiritual teachers have experience with Kundalini. They can offer guidance, energy work, or just a listening ear from a place of understanding. Just be discerning; there are charlatans out there.
  • Support groups: Finding others who have gone through this is invaluable. Knowing you're not alone, sharing stories, getting tips - it's incredibly validating.
  • Trusted friends or family: If you have someone who is open-minded and compassionate, even if they don't fully understand, having someone to just be with you can make a huge difference. Don't expect them to fix it, just ask them to listen.

I can't stress this enough: you need someone who won't dismiss your experiences. Someone who can hold space for the intensity of what you're going through without trying to "cure" it with conventional means. This isn't a conventional illness. It's a spiritual process with physical and psychological manifestations.

Surrender and Trust (Easier Said Than Done, I Know)

This is the hardest part. Your ego will fight tooth and nail against this process. It wants control, it wants predictability, it wants the old you back. But the truth is, you've lost control. The energy has taken over, and trying to fight it is like trying to stop a tsunami with a teacup. It just makes it worse.

  • Let go of expectations: There's no timeline for this. It could last months, or years. Just focus on getting through the next hour, the next day.
  • Practice acceptance: This doesn't mean you like it. It means you acknowledge that it's happening. "Okay, this is happening now. I'm feeling this. I'm seeing this." Without judgment.
  • Trust the process: This is where the "initiation" part comes in. This energy is intelligent. It knows what it's doing, even if you don't. It's clearing out what no longer serves you to make space for your true self. It's uncomfortable, but it's ultimately for your highest good.
  • Cultivate a sense of wonder (when you can): Amidst the terror, there are often moments of incredible beauty, insight, and connection. Try to lean into those moments, however fleeting. They're glimpses of what's on the other side.

I know what it's like to be curled up in a ball, begging for it to stop. But I'll tell you what, every time I surrendered, even for a moment, the intensity would often soften. It doesn't mean it goes away, but the resistance to it often creates more suffering than the process itself. You know what I mean?

The Service Part: What Comes After

So, you're going through hell. What's the point? Why you? This is where the "initiation into service" comes in. Once you've been through this fire, you're changed. You've seen things, felt things, understood things that most people haven't. You've developed an incredible capacity for empathy, for understanding suffering, for seeing beyond the veil of everyday reality.

You might find yourself drawn to helping others in ways you never imagined. Maybe you become a healer, a therapist, an artist, a teacher, an activist. Maybe you just become a more compassionate friend, a more understanding parent, a more present human being. The "service" doesn't have to be grand or public. It can be simply living from a place of deeper awareness and kindness.

Your unique experiences, your journey through the "shaman's sickness," gives you a wisdom that can't be learned from books. You've walked through the darkness and found your way back. That journey itself is a gift you can offer to the world, even if it's just by being a living example of resilience and transformation. You've been broken open, not broken apart. And that opening allows for a deeper connection to life, to others, and to your own true purpose.

It won't be easy. There will be setbacks. You'll still have moments of doubt and fear. But you'll also have moments of deep clarity, joy, and connection. You'll realize you're capable of so much more than you ever imagined. And that, my friend, is a power you can use to make a real difference, starting with your own life, and then radiating outwards. Does that land?

So yeah, you're not crazy. You're not alone. You're in a deep process of transformation. Hold on. Breathe. And know that on the other side of this storm, there's a stronger, wiser, and more compassionate you waiting to emerge. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
